data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "A pandemic corridor disease—likely equine-borne—decapitated palace systems by killing chariot horses and thus eliminating the coercive-communicative backbone of territorial control","mechanism": "Equine epizootic spread via Anatolian-Levantine trade routes c. 1220 BC; palatial states depended on chariotry for rapid suppression of periphery and elite prestige display; horse mortality forced either unsustainable bullion expenditure on replacement imports or aristocratic insubordination when prestige-campaign capacity collapsed; Egypt survived because Nubian and Libyan auxiliary infantry substituted for chariot dominance in its southern/western security architecture","scope": {"covers": ["Mycenaean Greece", "Hittite Anatolia", "Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"], "excludes": ["Egypt (core territory)", "Cyprus", "Mesopotamian polities"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Hittite and Mycenaean textual or zooarchaeological evidence shows normal horse mortality rates and continued chariot deployment up to destruction horizons", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian military records from Merneptah-Ramesses III show no shift from chariot to infantry predominance in campaign accounts", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "The Sea Peoples invasions are attested as naval and infantry assaults unconstrained by horse availability, yet palaces fall simultaneously to these same Sea Peoples", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Moderately atypical hybrid of biological and military-technological causation. Criticizable for positing invisible epizootic with no direct paleopathological evidence; explains Egyptian survival through military-structural difference rather than institutional redundancy."}
